title: Hearing - Sales and/or Use Tax (State,Local,MTA) - 202602011H
text:
SOAH DOCKET NO. 304-26-00707

CPA HEARING NO. 121,016

 



RE:      **************

TAXPAYER NO:  **************

AUDIT OFFICE:   **************

AUDIT PERIOD:   January 1, 2020 THROUGH December 31, 2022


Sales And Use Tax/RDT

BEFORE THE COMPTROLLER OF PUBLIC ACCOUNTS

OF THE STATE OF TEXAS


KELLY HANCOCK
Acting Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts


PETE SMITS

Representing Respondent


**************

Representing Petitioner


 



COMPTROLLER’S DECISION
 

This decision is considered final on March 2, 2026, unless a motion for rehearing is timely filed; this date of finality is calculated based on the Administrative Procedure Act (APA).[1]  The failure to timely file a motion for rehearing may result in adverse legal consequences.


Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) Kathy Pickup of the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH) issued a Proposal for Decision (PFD) that includes Findings of Fact and Conclusions of Law.  SOAH served the PFD on each party and each party was given an opportunity to file exceptions and replies with SOAH in accordance with SOAH’s rules of procedure.  The ALJ recommended that the Comptroller adopt the PFD as written.


After review and consideration, IT IS ORDERED that the PFD is adopted as written. 


The result from this Decision is Attachment A.  The ALJ’s recommendation letter is Attachment B.  The PFD as written is Attachment C.  Attachments A, B, and C are incorporated by reference.


Attachment A reflects a liability


The total sum of the tax, penalty, and interest is due and payable 20 days after a comptroller’s decision becomes final.[2]  If such sum is not timely paid, an additional penalty of 10 percent of the taxes due will accrue.


SIGNED on this 5th day of February 2026
 

KELLY HANCOCK
Acting Comptroller of Public Accounts


By: Lisa Craven

Deputy Comptroller


Attachment A, Texas Notification of Hearing Results
Attachment B, ALJ’s recommendation letter
Attachment C, Proposal for Decision as written



 


ATTACHMENT C

 

 

SOAH Docket No. 304-26-00707                                                             

Before the State Office of Administrative Hearings

TCPA Hearing No. 121,016

 

Proposal for Decision

 

************** (Petitioner) was audited for sales and use tax compliance by the Tax Division (Staff) of the Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts (Comptroller) and was assessed tax and interest. Penalty was waived. It requested redetermination, contending there was additional documentation available to establish that the audit erroneously included additional taxable purchases. Staff asserts Petitioner failed to provide any additional documentation or prove audit error. In this Proposal for Decision, the Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) recommends that the assessment against Petitioner be affirmed.

 

I. Notice, Jurisdiction, and Procedural History

 

Staff referred the contested case to the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH) and, on September 11, 2025, issued Petitioner a Notice of Hearing by Written Submission. On September 17, 2025, ALJ Kathy Pickup issued Order Setting Written Submission Requirements, which set the written submission hearing. Petitioner was represented by **************. Pete Smits represented Staff. The record closed November 24, 2025.

 

There are no issues of notice or jurisdiction; therefore, those matters are set out in the Findings of Fact and Conclusions of Law without further discussion here.

 

II. Reasons for Decision

 

A. Evidence Presented

 

Petitioner did not submit evidence or argument for the written submission hearing. Staff provided the pleadings the parties exchanged prior to referring the matter to SOAH and offered the following exhibits:


	
	Hearing Request Receipt;
	
	
	Hearing Grant Letter;
	
	
	Intro Letter;
	
	
	Texas Notification of Audit Results;
	
	
	Penalty and Interest Waiver Worksheet;
	
	
	Audit Report; and
	
	
	Audit Plan.
	


 

Staff’s exhibits were admitted into the record without objection.

 

B. Agreed Adjustments

 

There are no agreed adjustments.

 

C. Material Facts and Issues Presented

 

During the period at issue, Petitioner operated an excavating business that provided land clearing and improvement. Staff initiated a sales and use tax compliance audit of Petitioner’s business for the period January 1, 2020, through December 31, 2022. Petitioner provided the auditor with financial statements, trial balances, sales and purchase details, depreciation schedules, and purchase and sales invoices.

 

Petitioner was not permitted for sales and use tax during the audit period and, therefore, did not report taxable sales. The auditor completed a detailed examination, and adjustments were made for additional taxable sales (Exam 100) and taxable purchases (Exam 200).

 

On May 14, 2024, Staff issued a Texas Notification of Audit Results to Petitioner. The assessment included tax and interest. Penalty was waived. Petitioner timely requested redetermination, contending that it had additional documentation to establish that the audit erroneously included additional taxable purchases. On July 19, 2024, Staff issued Petitioner a letter requesting that Petitioner submit any additional documents, including resale and exemption certificates, to support its statement of grounds within 90 days from the date of the letter. Petitioner did not submit any documentation to support its contentions. Staff did not agree to adjust the audit and referred the matter to SOAH.

 

D. ALJ Analysis and Recommendation

 

Texas imposes a tax on each sale of a taxable item in this state. Tex. Tax Code § 151.051. The term “taxable item” includes tangible personal property and taxable services. Id. § 151.010. When tax is imposed on tangible personal property, the taxing entity’s prima facie burden of proof is easily met because, unless an exemption applies, all sales of tangible personal property in this state are taxable. Id.; see also Comptroller’s Decision No. 30,461 (1994). A taxpayer must produce sufficient records and supporting documentation appropriate to the tax or fee for the transactions in question to substantiate and enable verification of the taxpayer’s claim related to the amount of tax, penalty, or interest to be assessed, collected, or refunded. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(a).

 

Purchasers of taxable items are required to keep records in the form of receipts, shipping manifests, invoices, and other pertinent papers of all purchases of taxable items from every source made during each reporting period. Tex. Tax Code § 151.025(a)(2); 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.281(b)(2). A taxpayer is required to keep records to substantiate each claimed deduction or exclusion authorized by law. Tex. Tax Code § 151.025(a)(3). A taxpayer cannot defeat an assessment based on the burden of proof by not providing the necessary documents. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 117,981 (2023).

 

The auditor used Petitioner’s business records to assess liability for additional taxable sales and taxable purchases. An audit that is based on a taxpayer’s substantially complete books and records and conducted pursuant to established audit methodologies is entitled to a presumption of correctness, and Petitioner bears the burden of proof to demonstrate that the audit results are incorrect. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 110,187 (2017). For example, it is Petitioner’s burden to demonstrate, by a preponderance of evidence, that a scheduled purchase is nontaxable. See 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(e). A taxpayer is generally required to support its contentions with source records, and assertions in pleadings are insufficient to meet the burden of proof. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(a); see also Comptroller’s Decision No. 119,707 (2024).

 

Petitioner contends that the audit erroneously includes additional taxable purchases; however, it did not provide any documentation to support its contention. Bare assertions are insufficient to demonstrate error in the audit. See Baker v. Bullock, 529 S.W.2d 279 (Tex. Civ. App.—Austin 1975, writ ref’d n.r.e.); Comptroller’s Decision No. 114,880 (2019). Petitioner has not met its burden to establish audit error by a preponderance of the evidence. Therefore, the ALJ recommends affirming the audit.

 

III. Findings of Fact


	
	During the period at issue, ************** (Petitioner) operated an excavating business that provided land clearing and improvement.
	



	
	The Tax Division (Staff) of the Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts (Comptroller) initiated an audit to verify Petitioner’s compliance with Texas sales and use tax laws during the period January 1, 2020, through December 31, 2022.
	
	
	Petitioner provided financial statements, trial balances, sales and purchase details, depreciation schedules, and purchase and sales invoices.
	
	
	Petitioner was not permitted for sales and use tax during the audit period and, therefore, did not report taxable sales.
	
	
	The auditor completed a detailed examination, and adjustments were made for additional taxable sales (Exam 100) and taxable purchases (Exam 200).
	
	
	On May 14, 2024, Staff issued a Texas Notification of Audit Results to Petitioner. The assessment included tax and interest. Penalty was waived.
	
	
	After receiving the assessment, Petitioner timely requested redetermination.
	
	
	On July 19, 2024, Staff issued Petitioner a letter requesting that Petitioner submit any additional documents, including resale and exemption certificates, to support its statement of grounds within 90 days from the date of the letter.
	
	
	On September 11, 2025, Staff issued a Notice of Hearing by Written Submission to Petitioner. The notice contained a statement of the nature of the hearing; a statement of the legal authority and jurisdiction under which the hearing was to be held; a reference to the particular sections of the statutes and rules involved; and a short, plain statement of the factual matters asserted, or an attachment that incorporated by reference the factual matters asserted in the complaint or petition filed with the state agency.
	
	
	On September 17, 2025, Administrative Law Judge Kathy Pickup issued an Order Setting Written Submission Requirements, which set the written submission hearing and filing deadlines.
	
	
	The contested case record closed on November 24, 2025.
	
	
	Petitioner did not provide evidence or argument for the hearing.
	


 

IV. Conclusions of Law


	
	The Comptroller has jurisdiction over this matter. Tex. Tax Code ch. 111.
	
	
	SOAH has jurisdiction over matters related to the hearing, including the authority to issue a proposal for decision with findings of fact and conclusions of law. Tex. Gov’t Code ch. 2003.
	
	
	Staff provided a proper and timely notice of hearing. Tex. Gov’t Code ch. 2001; Tex. Tax Code § 111.009.
	
	
	Texas imposes a tax on each sale of a taxable item in this state. Tex. Tax Code § 151.051.
	
	
	The term “taxable item” includes tangible personal property and taxable services. Tex. Tax Code § 151.010.
	
	
	When tax is imposed on tangible personal property, the taxing entity’s prima facie burden of proof is easily met because, unless an exemption applies, all sales of tangible personal property in this state are taxable. Tex. Tax Code § 151.010; see also Comptroller’s Decision No. 30,461 (1994).
	
	
	A taxpayer must produce sufficient records and supporting documentation appropriate to the tax or fee for the transactions in question to substantiate and enable verification of the taxpayer’s claim related to the amount of tax, penalty, or interest to be assessed, collected, or refunded. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(a).
	
	
	Purchasers of taxable items are required to keep records in the form of receipts, shipping manifests, invoices, and other pertinent papers of all purchases of taxable items from every source made during each reporting period. Tex. Tax Code § 151.025(a)(2); 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.281(b)(2).
	
	
	A taxpayer is required to keep records to substantiate each claimed deduction or exclusion authorized by law. Tex. Tax Code § 151.025(a)(3).
	



	
	A taxpayer cannot defeat an assessment based on the burden of proof by not providing the necessary documents. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 117,981 (2023).
	
	
	An audit that is based on a taxpayer’s substantially complete books and records and conducted pursuant to established audit methodologies is entitled to a presumption of correctness, and Petitioner bears the burden of proof to demonstrate that the audit results are incorrect. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 110,187 (2017).
	
	
	It is Petitioner’s burden to demonstrate, by a preponderance of evidence, that a scheduled purchase is nontaxable. See 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(e).
	
	
	A taxpayer is generally required to support its contentions with source records, and assertions in pleadings are insufficient to meet the burden of proof. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(a); see also Comptroller’s Decision No. 119,707 (2024).
	
	
	It is Petitioner’s burden to show audit error by a preponderance of the evidence. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(e).
	
	
	Bare assertions are insufficient to demonstrate error in the audit. See Baker v. Bullock, 529 S.W.2d 279 (Tex. Civ. App.—Austin 1975, writ ref’d n.r.e.); Comptroller’s Decision No. 114,880 (2019).
	
	
	Petitioner has not met its burden to establish audit error by a preponderance of the evidence.
	
	
	The audit assessment against Petitioner should be affirmed.
	


 

Signed November 25, 2025

 

Kathy Pickup

Presiding Administrative Law Judge

 



 

 


ENDNOTES



[1] The date calculated is 25 days after this decision is signed. See APA, Tex. Gov’t Code § 2001.146(a); S.B. 1095, Acts 2017, 85th Leg.   For additional guidance, refer to the Frequently Asked Questions Related to Motions for Rehearing, found here: http://comptroller.texas.gov/taxes/publications/96-1789.pdf



[2] See Tex. Tax Code § 111.0081(c).
subjects: PURCHASER-FAILURE TO KEEP RECORDS TO SUBSTANTIATE DEDUCTIONS OR EXCLUSIONS OF TAXABLE ITEMS PURCHASED
BARE ASSERTIONS IN PLEADINGS ARE INSUFFICIENT TO MEET THE BURDEN OF PROOF-MUST BE SUPPORTED BY BUSINESS RECORDS
